subject: Human Performance
learning_objective: Understand the physiological effects of flight on the human body and be able to recognize the signs of hypoxia, hyperventilation, fatigue, and other factors affecting PPL pilot performance.

concepts.title: Hypobaric Hypoxia
concepts.content: Hypoxia is a lack of oxygen in body tissues due to decreased partial pressure of oxygen at altitude. First symptoms appear from 10,000 ft without supplemental oxygen: euphoria, tunnel vision, reduced coordination. The pilot is unaware of their own degradation.
common_errors: Failing to recognize one's own hypoxia symptoms
Believing you can stay above 10,000 ft for more than 30 minutes without risk
Confusing hypoxia with fatigue

concepts.title: Time of Useful Consciousness
concepts.content: Time of Useful Consciousness (TUC) is the duration a pilot can perform useful tasks after oxygen deprivation. At 18,000 ft, TUC is 20 to 30 minutes. At 25,000 ft, it drops to 2 to 5 minutes. At 30,000 ft, it is 30 to 60 seconds.
common_errors: Underestimating how quickly consciousness is lost at altitude
Not preparing oxygen before reaching critical altitude

concepts.title: Hyperventilation
concepts.content: Hyperventilation is breathing too fast or too deep, lowering blood CO2 levels. Symptoms resemble hypoxia: dizziness, tingling, spasms, blurred vision. Unlike hypoxia, hyperventilation is caused by stress or anxiety, not altitude.
common_errors: Confusing hyperventilation with hypoxia
Continuing to breathe fast thinking you need more air
Not knowing the paper bag breathing technique

concepts.title: Ear Barotrauma
concepts.content: Barotrauma is pain caused by a pressure difference between the middle ear and the outside. It occurs mainly during descent when the middle ear does not vent quickly enough. The Valsalva maneuver (pinch nose and blow gently) helps equalize pressure.
common_errors: Descending too fast without equalizing ears
Ignoring ear pain and continuing the descent
Confusing barotrauma with an ENT infection

concepts.title: Sinus and Facial Barotrauma
concepts.content: Sinuses can also suffer barotrauma when congested (cold, allergy). Pain is localized to the forehead or cheekbones. A pilot with a cold should avoid flying: the pain can become incapacitating and sinus rupture may occur.
common_errors: Flying with a cold thinking 'it's just a stuffy nose'
Using decongestants without knowing their side effects (drowsiness)

concepts.title: In-Flight Vision and Visual Illusions
concepts.content: Night vision is provided by rods, which take 30 minutes to adapt to darkness. White light destroys this adaptation instantly. On night approach, false runway illusions and 'black hole approach' are common. Hypoxia worsens peripheral vision degradation.
common_errors: Looking at a white light during night flight then seeing nothing
Underestimating dark adaptation time (30 min)
Not knowing the 'black hole approach' illusion

concepts.title: Vestibular Illusion and False Attitude Sensation
concepts.content: The inner ear detects accelerations but not constant speeds. In flight without external visibility, the pilot may feel a false attitude sensation (e.g., a slow turn goes unnoticed, then a recovery feels like a climb when level). The only reliable reference is the instrument panel.
common_errors: Trusting sensations rather than instruments
Not knowing the 'stop the turn' reflex when in doubt

concepts.title: Spatial Disorientation
concepts.content: Spatial disorientation occurs when inner ear sensations contradict the instruments. The pilot may believe they are climbing when actually descending. The golden rule: when in doubt, trust the instruments, never your sensations. Entering IMC without training is the leading cause of fatal accidents in general aviation.
common_errors: Attempting to fly in IMC without qualification
Correcting a sensation with an action contrary to the instruments
Panicking and losing control when entering clouds

concepts.title: Pilot Fatigue
concepts.content: Fatigue reduces cognitive and physical performance: increased reaction time, judgment errors, micro-sleeps. Common causes in light aviation: long flight without a break, jet lag, short night, dehydration. Fatigue is cumulative and the pilot is a poor judge of their own fatigue level.
common_errors: Thinking 'I'm tired but I can still fly'
Not planning a break on a long flight
Confusing fatigue with laziness

concepts.title: I'M SAFE — Pre-flight Self-Assessment
concepts.content: The I'M SAFE acronym is a pre-flight self-assessment checklist: Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol (12h bottle-to-throttle, 24h recommended), Fatigue, Eating. A single 'yes' to any item may justify canceling the flight.
common_errors: Flying with a cold or under medication without checking effects
Underestimating the effect of stress or fatigue
Not knowing the 12-hour alcohol rule

concepts.title: Alcohol and Flying
concepts.content: EASA regulations prohibit flying less than 12 hours after the last alcohol consumption (bottle-to-throttle). Maximum allowed blood alcohol level is 0.2 g/L. Even below this threshold, residual alcohol affects judgment and coordination for up to 24 hours. Hangover worsens fatigue and dehydration at altitude.
common_errors: Thinking that after 12h you're 'good to fly' without considering residual effects
Confusing 'bottle-to-throttle' (12h) with 'zero alcohol before flying'

concepts.title: Dehydration in Flight
concepts.content: The air at altitude is dry and cabin ventilation accelerates dehydration. Symptoms: headache, fatigue, cramps, decreased concentration. Drinking water regularly during flight is essential. Coffee and sodas worsen dehydration due to caffeine and sugar.
common_errors: Drinking only coffee during a long flight
Waiting until thirsty to drink (thirst is a late sign)

concepts.title: Hypoglycemia in Flight
concepts.content: Low blood sugar causes fatigue, tremors, concentration difficulties, and irritability. Pilots who skip a meal before a flight or fly long without snacks are at risk. Carrying snacks (granola bars, fruit) in the cabin is a simple and effective precaution.
common_errors: Flying on an empty stomach to 'save time'
Not planning food for a flight longer than 2 hours

concepts.title: Medication and Fitness to Fly
concepts.content: Many over-the-counter medications (antihistamines, decongestants, sleeping pills) affect alertness and are incompatible with flying. Even a medication prescribed by a doctor may be prohibited in flight. The pilot must check compatibility with their AME (AeroMedical Examiner) before flying under treatment.
common_errors: Taking medication without reading the 'driving vehicles' warning
Thinking an over-the-counter drug is automatically compatible with flying

concepts.title: Stress and In-Flight Performance
concepts.content: Moderate stress improves performance (Yerkes-Dodson law). Excessive stress degrades it: attention tunneling, forgotten procedures, impulsive decisions. Stress management techniques include: controlled breathing, task prioritization, use of checklists, and clear communication with ATC.
common_errors: Letting stress build up without using breathing techniques
Stopping communication with ATC when stressed
Trying to do everything at once instead of prioritizing

concepts.title: Gas Expansion at Altitude (Boyle's Law)
concepts.content: Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, causing gas expansion in body cavities (stomach, intestines). This can cause abdominal pain, gas, and discomfort that distracts the pilot. Avoid carbonated drinks and heavy meals before flying.
common_errors: Drinking carbonated beverages before a flight at altitude
Eating a heavy meal just before flying

concepts.title: Cold and Hypothermia in Flight
concepts.content: Cabin temperature decreases with altitude (approximately 2°C per 1,000 ft). Wind chill worsens the feeling of cold. Hypothermia reduces dexterity, reaction time, and judgment. The pilot should dress in layers and carry warm clothing even in summer.
common_errors: Underestimating the cold at altitude in summer
Not bringing a jacket or gloves for a long flight

operational_rules.title: I'M SAFE Self-Assessment Before Every Flight
operational_rules.procedure: Before each flight, mentally run the I'M SAFE checklist: Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol (12h bottle-to-throttle), Fatigue, Eating. If any single item is positive, postpone the flight.
conditions: Before a long or night flight
common_errors: Skipping this checklist when in a hurry
Lying to yourself about your actual condition
safety_notes: I'M SAFE is the first safety barrier even before the pre-flight inspection
A tired or sick pilot is more dangerous than an aircraft with a minor fault

operational_rules.title: Procedure for Suspected Hypoxia
operational_rules.procedure: If you suspect hypoxia (tunnel vision, euphoria, reduced coordination): 1) Put on oxygen immediately if available, 2) Descend urgently below 10,000 ft, 3) Inform ATC of the emergency descent, 4) Once at low altitude, assess whether the flight can continue or a landing is required.
conditions: In case of unexplained symptoms at altitude
common_errors: Delaying descent thinking 'it's not serious'
Forgetting to inform ATC of the emergency descent
safety_notes: Hypoxia symptoms are insidious — the pilot is the last to recognize them
A passenger can be a good indicator: ask them regularly how they feel

operational_rules.title: Managing Hyperventilation
operational_rules.procedure: If you feel dizziness, tingling, or blurred vision in a stressful situation: 1) Speak out loud or count slowly to slow your breathing, 2) Breathe into cupped hands or a paper bag if available, 3) Focus on slow breathing (inhale 4s, exhale 6s), 4) If symptoms persist, descend and land.
conditions: In a stressful or anxious situation in flight
In case of symptoms similar to hypoxia but at low altitude
common_errors: Continuing to hyperventilate thinking you need more air
Confusing with hypoxia and using oxygen (ineffective)
safety_notes: Hyperventilation is often triggered by a real emergency — treat the emergency first, then the breathing
An instructor on board can guide the student's breathing

operational_rules.title: Preventing Barotrauma During Descent
operational_rules.procedure: During descent, equalize ears regularly using the Valsalva maneuver (pinch nose, close mouth, blow gently). If pain persists: reduce the rate of descent, or even climb slightly to re-equalize, then descend more slowly. Never force a descent with ear pain.
conditions: In case of a cold or sinusitis
For pilots prone to barotraumatic ear infections
common_errors: Descending too fast without equalizing ears
Ignoring the pain and continuing the descent
safety_notes: A yawn or swallowing can be enough to equalize ears without Valsalva
Children and people with colds are at higher risk

operational_rules.title: 12-Hour Alcohol Rule
operational_rules.procedure: Respect a minimum of 12 hours between the last alcohol consumption and taking command (bottle-to-throttle). Blood alcohol must not exceed 0.2 g/L. For a flight the day after a heavy drinking session, wait 24 hours. When in doubt, do not fly.
conditions: After alcohol consumption the previous day
common_errors: Thinking that after 12h you are fit even with a hangover
Underestimating the effect of residual alcohol on judgment
safety_notes: Alcohol affects coordination and judgment long after blood alcohol has dropped
Dehydration from alcohol is worsened at altitude

operational_rules.title: Managing Spatial Disorientation
operational_rules.procedure: If you feel a sensation contradicting the instruments: 1) Trust the instruments, never your sensations, 2) Stabilize the attitude by looking at the attitude indicator, 3) Contact ATC and declare an emergency if necessary, 4) Request a heading or radar assistance to reach VMC conditions. Do not attempt abrupt maneuvers.
conditions: During night flight without visual references
When feeling a 'stall' or 'turn' sensation not confirmed by instruments
common_errors: Correcting a sensation with an action contrary to the instruments
Delaying contact with ATC thinking 'it will sort itself out'
safety_notes: Spatial disorientation kills more pilots than engine failures in general aviation
Regular instrument flight training (even basic) saves lives

operational_rules.title: Hydration and Nutrition in Flight
operational_rules.procedure: Carry at least 1 liter of water per person for a flight longer than one hour. Drink small regular sips, do not wait until thirsty. Carry snacks (granola bars, dried fruit) for flights over 2 hours. Avoid coffee and sodas in large quantities.
common_errors: Drinking only coffee during the flight
Skipping breakfast before a morning flight
safety_notes: Dehydration worsens the effects of hypoxia and fatigue
A well-hydrated and well-fed pilot makes better decisions

summary: Pilot physiology is a determining factor in flight safety. Hypoxia, hyperventilation, spatial disorientation, fatigue, stress, alcohol, and medications are silent threats that degrade performance without the pilot realizing it. The I'M SAFE checklist before each flight, absolute trust in instruments when in doubt, and good lifestyle habits (hydration, nutrition, sleep) are the three pillars of prevention. A physically and mentally healthy pilot is the best safety equipment on board.
